frameworks.name: Marketing Moonshot Framework
frameworks.definition: A two-category model separating standard-optimization activity (80% of bees following the waggle dance) from exploratory high-variance bets (20% rogue bees) where failure is frequent but rare successes pay back 10,000-fold; applied to marketing as changing perception rather than physical reality to achieve order-of-magnitude improvements at relatively low cost.

frameworks.name: Satisficing vs. Optimizing Decision Model
frameworks.definition: Herbert Simon's distinction between archery-style optimization (aim for the single best outcome) and darts-style satisficing (aim for the southwestern quadrant to avoid catastrophic low scores); in evolutionary and business contexts, satisficing considers both average outcome and worst-case variance, not only the maximum.

frameworks.name: Costly Signaling / Handicap Theory
frameworks.definition: Amotz Zahavi's principle that meaningful signals require genuine cost (financial, effort, time, creativity) because cheap signals are unreliable; applied to marketing, advertising spend, gift-giving, and brand behavior as mechanisms that signal long-term commitment and trustworthiness to observers.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that a narrow focus on a single metric for too long is nearly always disastrous, because people on the other end learn to game the system. He cites UK National Health Service GPs who refuse to accept advance appointment bookings — scoring 100% on the metric of same-day appointments — while patients are kept in ambulances outside hospitals to avoid the metric of time-to-admission after entry.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies to any organization where a single KPI is measured and enforced as a performance standard
claims.mechanism: When one metric is fixed as the optimization target, rational actors redirect effort toward satisfying the metric rather than the underlying goal, because metric performance is rewarded and underlying goal performance is not independently tracked.
bounded_by: Does not apply when metrics are regularly rotated or when multiple complementary metrics create redundant coverage

claims.text: Rory Sutherland contends that psychological improvements to a product or service are comparatively easier to achieve at 10x scale than engineering improvements, because the laws of physics constrain engineering gains while human psychology remains poorly understood and therefore poorly exploited. Making a train journey 10 times more enjoyable or productive may be achievable at a fraction of the cost of making it 10 times faster.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies where the primary constraint on improvement is physical or engineering feasibility rather than human perception
claims.mechanism: The opportunity for 10x improvement is greatest where existing knowledge is weakest; engineering and physics are well-mapped, so marginal gains are hard; psychology is poorly mapped, so large gains remain available.
bounded_by: Does not apply where psychological perceptions are already well-optimized or where physical minimum thresholds still need to be met

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that economists' assumption of perfect information and perfect trust in all human transactions is the foundational reason finance departments distrust marketing. In an economist's model of the world, marketing is at best a cost to minimize (because in a world of perfect information, buyers already know the value) and at worst ethically suspect (as a distortion of rational preference).
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies to organizations where financial decision-makers hold neoclassical economic assumptions
claims.mechanism: If perfect information and trust are axiomatic, then marketing cannot add value — value is already known — so any marketing spend is either waste or manipulation from the CFO's perspective.
bounded_by: Austrian economists (von Mises, Hayek) rejected this axiom; organizations schooled in behavioral economics hold different assumptions

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that all value is subjective and is as much a product of the context in which something is perceived as of what something actually is. He cites Nespresso pods: if sold as a jar of coffee by equivalent weight, they would cost approximately 45 pounds and appear absurdly expensive; sold as a 29p pod, the reference frame shifts to Starbucks (2.30 pounds per cup), making the pod feel almost free.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies to any product whose unit of sale can be compared to multiple reference categories with different price norms
claims.mechanism: The perceived value of a purchase is determined by which reference category the buyer's brain uses for comparison; repackaging a product into a smaller unit shifts the reference category from bulk commodity to premium single-serve, changing the price benchmark without changing the physical product.

numbers.axis: equivalent coffee cost by packaging format

bounded_by: Does not override reference-price anchoring when the buyer is a price-sensitive expert who calculates per-unit cost independently

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that humans do not perceive the world objectively because evolutionary fitness, not accuracy, governs the nervous system. If evolution can gain 2% extra fitness by sacrificing 20% of perceptual accuracy, it will make that trade-off, because evolution selects for survival and reproductive fitness, not truth.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Natural selection optimizes for organism survival and reproduction; perceptual accuracy is instrumentally useful only insofar as it contributes to those goals. Where a slightly distorted perception (e.g., resolving ambiguity faster) improves survival odds, evolution encodes the distortion.

numbers.axis: evolutionary trade-off between fitness gain and perceptual accuracy

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that the reduction of uncertainty — knowing when something will happen — is more valuable to human wellbeing than an equivalent improvement in the underlying metric. London Underground dot-matrix arrival displays increased passenger satisfaction more per pound spent than any physical improvement (faster trains, more frequent service, better rolling stock), because people prefer waiting 9 minutes with certainty over waiting 6 minutes in uncertainty.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies in contexts where the service timeline is opaque to the consumer
claims.mechanism: Uncertainty activates a chronic stress state that compounds over time; a known wait, even if longer, allows the brain to cease monitoring and return to baseline, reducing total psychological cost.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the actual wait substantially exceeds communicated wait (the promise is broken)

claims.text: Rory Sutherland contends that premium brands earn a price premium not primarily because consumers believe branded goods are intrinsically better on average, but because consumers believe they are less likely to be disappointing. A brand with a large reputation has more to lose from selling a bad product than an unknown; therefore, brand is a guarantee of low variance, not high average.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies in markets where product quality is difficult to verify before purchase
claims.mechanism: Buyers satisfice — minimizing worst-case regret as much as maximizing expected outcome. A brand with large reputational capital has strong incentives to avoid selling poor-quality products, so the probability of a catastrophically bad outcome is lower. Buyers pay the brand premium to purchase variance reduction, not average elevation.
bounded_by: Does not apply to brand-insensitive buyers who make purely analytical purchase decisions
Does not apply where product quality is fully verifiable pre-purchase

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that McDonald's is the world's most popular restaurant chain not because it has the highest average food quality, but because it has the lowest variance — it is the most reliable at not being bad. Michelin-starred restaurants have a higher average quality but greater variance: greater chance of disappointment, greater chance of feeling ripped off, and even greater chance of food-safety problems.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: In consumer decision-making, avoiding catastrophic outcomes (variance minimization) is weighted more heavily than maximizing average outcomes; a brand that delivers consistent mediocrity outperforms one with occasional brilliance and occasional disasters in terms of repeat purchase.
bounded_by: Does not apply to consumers who prioritize aspirational experiences and are willing to accept variance for the possibility of excellence

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that in business settings, decision-makers shift from regret minimization (acting for themselves) to blame minimization (acting to avoid being held responsible), because individual interests diverge from organizational interests. As a result, they default to economically conventional choices where the algorithm or consensus provides cover: no one gets fired for booking Heathrow to JFK, even when London City to Newark would be faster.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies in organizational contexts where the decision-maker is not the principal and can shift blame to a consensus or algorithm
claims.mechanism: When a decision deviates from the conventional choice and fails, the individual decision-maker bears all the blame. When the conventional choice is followed and fails, the blame diffuses to market conditions, the vendor, or the algorithm. This asymmetry creates a structural bias toward conventionality, regardless of expected outcome.
bounded_by: Does not apply to owner-operators where decision-maker and principal are the same person
Does not apply in cultures with explicit psychological-safety norms around innovation

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that research in advertising often functions as career protection (arse covering) rather than decision-making input, because the research findings do not change subsequent spending decisions — whether ROI is measured at 5 or 1.2, the budget allocation remains the same. This is evidence that the purpose of the research is to secure the researcher's position, not to improve outcomes.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies when research is commissioned in advance of a decision already made
bounded_by: Does not apply in organizations with pre-registered research protocols or where spending is genuinely contingent on research findings

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that a reframe of reference is worth approximately 80 IQ points, quoting a figure from behind the Apollo program. The corollary is that many successful entrepreneurs and marketers appear unintelligent because intelligence at problem-solving matters less than the ability to change the frame of reference through which the problem is defined.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland

anchors.region: same person with superior frame of reference

bounded_by: The 80 IQ figure is a heuristic attributed to an Apollo-era figure, not a peer-reviewed measurement

claims.text: Rory Sutherland contends that the most successful competitive response to Coca-Cola in 150 years is Red Bull — which is smaller, more expensive, and rated in blind taste tests as the worst-tasting carbonated beverage the testing company had ever evaluated. This falsifies the economic prediction that the winning alternative should be tastier, cheaper, and larger.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: The human unconscious associates medicinal or psychotropic efficacy with unusual or unpleasant taste; a product that tastes too pleasant is not believed to be powerful. Red Bull's tiny can, high price, and bad taste all signal scarcity and potency, activating the brain's medicinal-product schema.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that putting a 20%-lower-fat label on a biscuit causes the product to taste worse in consumer perception, even though blind taste tests show no detectable difference. The labeling changes the psychological frame, and humans cannot compartmentalize the knowledge from the sensory experience — perception is a blurry composite of all available signals.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies when packaging or labeling provides information that is schema-inconsistent with high sensory pleasure
claims.mechanism: Human perception integrates multi-channel signals into a unified experience rather than processing them independently; a cue that signals reduced-indulgence (lower fat) primes the taste system to perceive reduced richness, even when the food is physically identical.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that branded painkillers are measurably more effective than unbranded equivalents even in double-blind conditions — EEG measurements show reduced pain signaling when subjects believe they are taking an expensive branded painkiller. The effect works even without the drug, and even when measured physiologically rather than via self-report.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Price and brand signals modulate expectation, which in turn modulates the neurological pain-processing pathway; higher expected efficacy triggers a stronger expectation-mediated analgesic response, measurable via electrode-based brain monitoring.
bounded_by: Effect magnitude is not quantified by Sutherland; specific effect sizes require clinical citation

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that meaningful signaling — in marketing, relationships, and commerce — requires genuine cost, because costless signals are unreliable. He applies Amotz Zahavi's handicap theory: the peacock's tail is costly and useless for survival, which is precisely what makes it a reliable signal of genetic fitness. Similarly, an expensive launch advertising campaign signals genuine belief in long-term product success, because no one would commit that spending to a product expected to fail.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies in any context where the signaler has private information about quality that cannot be directly verified by the receiver
claims.mechanism: If a signal is cheap to produce, it can be faked by low-quality actors; if it is costly, only actors who genuinely expect future payoff can afford to send it. Receivers who know this logic infer quality from cost of communication, not content of communication.
bounded_by: Does not apply where signal cost is not visible to the receiver
Does not apply where the receiver does not understand or process costly signaling heuristics

claims.text: Rory Sutherland contends that the way digital advertising is currently measured — optimizing only what happens before and immediately at the point of purchase — systematically undervalues post-purchase signaling (such as Selfridges packaging or Five Guys extra fries) that communicates long-term relationship intent. Combined with shareholder-value pressure for short-term profit, this creates businesses that appear psychopathic to customers: all communications are self-interested.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies to digital-native businesses optimizing on last-click or near-purchase attribution models
claims.mechanism: When post-purchase behavior cannot be measured or attributed, it is not rewarded in the business system; rational managers then eliminate it. But consumers interpret post-purchase effort as continuation probability — evidence that the business intends a long-term relationship — and absence of such signals shifts trust downward.
bounded_by: Does not apply to purely transactional purchases where no ongoing relationship is expected

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that contactless payment makes purchases feel approximately 15% cheaper than chip-and-pin payment for the same item, because removing the physical act of entering a PIN reduces the psychological pain of payment.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies to consumer retail purchase contexts comparing payment modalities

numbers.axis: perceived price reduction by payment method

bounded_by: Specific figure cited without peer-reviewed source; treat as illustrative magnitude rather than precise measurement

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that innovation in large companies fails because the standard operating environment requires all employees to optimize within the same metric system, making exploratory behavior appear irrational. IBM produced the personal computer by sending its most unconventional employee to Boca Raton, Florida — deliberately 2,000 miles from headquarters — to operate outside the dominant optimization system.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies to large organizations with dominant metric-reporting cultures
claims.mechanism: When a single optimization standard governs all activity, any expenditure of effort in a non-standard direction appears wasteful by definition; the only escape is structural separation of exploratory from exploitative work.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that where one has bounded downside and large upside — testing counterintuitive marketing hypotheses — the rational strategy is to run experiments that competitors will not run, because conventional competitors are all testing the same economically orthodox ideas. Discovering a counterintuitive success produces exclusive advantage, while failure merely confirms what competitors already assumed.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies when testing is cheap relative to the potential upside of a successful counterintuitive hypothesis
applies when competitors are systematically avoiding the same class of tests
bounded_by: Does not apply if the counterintuitive hypothesis is already well-known and tested in adjacent industries

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that for a product that is not selling, a marketer should first try raising the price before lowering it, because low price can itself be the barrier to purchase — implausibly low pricing signals hidden defects or low social status of ownership. If the price increase fails, it can be reversed; if the price decrease fails, the lower reference price anchors future negotiations downward.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies when a product is not selling despite apparent value — i.e., sales failure is not attributable to physical inaccessibility or awareness
claims.mechanism: Price functions as a quality and social-fitness signal; implausibly low pricing activates a defect-detection heuristic in buyers, reducing purchase probability below what a higher price would produce.
bounded_by: Does not apply in commodity markets where price is the dominant and transparent competitive variable
Does not apply for buyers with full information about true production costs

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that scarcity signals — such as airline websites showing only five seats remaining at a price — work even on people who are fully aware of the technique and its psychological mechanism. He reports that after 28 years of studying behavioral science and knowing exactly what the scarcity prompt is trying to do, he still clicks.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Scarcity processing appears to operate through an automatic (System 1) pathway that is not deactivated by metacognitive awareness; knowing about the manipulation does not override the behavioral response it triggers.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland contends that delayed prescriptions reduce unnecessary antibiotic consumption by approximately an order of magnitude, because 67% of patients given a delayed (post-dated) prescription do not cash it in, and of those who do, a significant fraction do not take the full course — exploiting sunk-cost bias and natural recovery rather than explicit instruction.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: applies in primary care settings for non-severe acute conditions where watchful waiting is medically appropriate
claims.mechanism: Sunk-cost bias causes patients who fill an immediate prescription to take the antibiotics even when symptoms have resolved (they paid, so they use). A delayed prescription does not activate sunk cost until the patient actively chooses to fill it; by then, most have recovered and choose not to.

anchors.region: patients given delayed prescription who do not fill it
patients given immediate prescription who do not fill it

bounded_by: Does not apply to severe bacterial infections requiring immediate treatment
Aggregate effect depends on the fraction of prescribed antibiotics that are genuinely unnecessary

examples.text: Rory Sutherland describes an Australian stunt by comedians Hamish and Andy: they set up a legitimate Ed Sheeran peep show charging two Australian dollars, with Sheeran sitting on a stool playing songs. The setup looked like an adult venue; despite 50+ minutes of trying, almost no one entered. Even when a couple finally did enter, they were convinced to go in only after scarcity signals were added: told it would get busy later and they would only get 30 seconds. The example illustrates that a product with genuine value (Ed Sheeran performing live for 2 dollars) can completely fail to sell when its framing and context undermine trust — the product was priced too cheaply and placed in a low-trust venue, both of which signaled defect. The fix would have been to raise the price and reframe the context.

Rory Sutherland describes the Rolls-Royce and Maserati repositioning: these manufacturers stopped exhibiting at car shows and began exhibiting at boat and plane shows. At a car show, a 300,000-euro car looks expensive. At an event where attendees have been looking at Learjets all morning, a 300,000-euro car feels like an impulse buy. Same product, same price, entirely different perceived value — demonstrating that reference frame, not product attributes, determines perceived expensiveness.

Rory Sutherland describes an EasyJet pilot who reframed the experience of being bused from plane to terminal. Rather than leaving passengers to form their own negative conclusion (a bus means a cheaper airport experience), the pilot announced: bad news, a plane is blocking our gate so there is no air bridge; good news, the bus will take you all the way to passport control so you will not have to walk through the terminal with your luggage. The information (the bus goes to passport control) was always true; no one had ever stated it. Once stated, passengers were able to frame the bus positively. Sutherland argues that all the material for synthesizing happiness was always present — the absence of communication left buyers unable to construct a positive frame.

Rory Sutherland describes the Philips Air Fryer case: in Europe the product sells poorly because the name triggers avoidance among exactly the target audience — health-conscious consumers who are trying to eat less fried food. In Asia, where frying is the dominant cooking method and there is no cultural stigma around it, the same product sells strongly. A colleague suggested renaming it in French as the Air Sauters — because framing any cooking process in French elevates its perceived sophistication and eliminates the fried-food avoidance response. Same physical product, different name, entirely different market outcome.

Rory Sutherland cites experiments in which heavy drinkers are given gin and tonic, then for the rest of an evening given tonic water in a glass whose rim has been dipped in gin. Not only do the drinkers not detect the substitution — they become intoxicated. The brain, having registered eight apparent gin and tonics, begins producing the physiological and behavioral response associated with that quantity of alcohol, independent of actual alcohol intake. This demonstrates that the behavioral and physiological response to a substance can be driven by contextual signals (ritual, container, rim dipping) rather than the substance itself.

Rory Sutherland describes how Uber is primarily a psychological rather than a logistical improvement over traditional taxi services. Standard taxi metrics focus on reducing objective wait time in minutes; reducing wait time requires expensive excess capacity (cabs driving around empty). Uber instead provides a real-time map showing the cab's location. This does not reduce the objective wait time; it eliminates the uncertainty of that wait. The result is that a passenger waiting nine minutes for an Uber — while watching the map — feels less distress than someone waiting six minutes for a traditional cab without information about its location. The most valuable product change was psychological, not operational.

Rory Sutherland uses Selfridges' post-purchase experience as an example of costly signaling for continuation probability. After a customer has already paid, Selfridges delivers the product in an expensive branded box lined with tissue paper, with a decorative sticker affirming the purchase, followed by an email confirming delivery and expressing care. None of this changes what the customer received; all of it is discretionary post-purchase investment that signals the retailer intends a long-term relationship. Sutherland contrasts this with businesses that invest exclusively in pre-purchase communication and disappear immediately after payment — which communicates psychopathic short-term orientation to customers.

Rory Sutherland describes the Five Guys extra fries example: when a customer orders any size of fries, the cup is filled to the top as legally required, then an additional large scoop is thrown into the bag. This post-purchase addition costs Five Guys almost nothing but communicates continued generosity after the transaction has closed. Sutherland calls this continuation probability signaling — evidence to the customer that the business is operating under a long-term, repeatedly-interacting relationship model rather than a one-off extraction model.

Rory Sutherland argues that a bumblebee preferring flowers that smell of a specific chemical is a reliable signal of nectar availability because the chemical is a necessary ingredient for nectar production, not an incidental one. Any flower that produces that smell genuinely has nectar, because faking the signal is physically impossible without the underlying resource. He applies this to engagement rings: the monetary cost must hurt to be meaningful, because anyone can say words of commitment; spending money that genuinely hurts demonstrates skin in the game and makes the signal reliable.

practices.text: Create a separate budget and separate organizational space for exploratory (20%) activity with a far higher tolerance of failure than the standard optimization (80%) budget; evaluate exploratory bets on overall probabilistic gains, not individual success rates.

Test counterintuitive hypotheses specifically because competitors will not; all other businesses are testing economically orthodox ideas, so testing the same class of hypotheses produces no information advantage.

When a product is not selling, raise the price before lowering it; low price can itself be the barrier to purchase by signaling defect or low social status, and a price increase can be reversed if it fails.

Never present unqualified bad news; always embed a piece of positive information alongside negative news to give the recipient the material with which to construct a positive frame (the fox-and-grapes reframe).

When measuring advertising and marketing ROI, include post-purchase investments in continuation probability signaling (packaging, follow-up communications, discretionary extras) rather than only pre-purchase and point-of-purchase spend.
